advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a hard timeout=/kill to the blocking subprocess.run calls (or run reviews in a killable child process) so the wait_for timeout actually terminates the hung work rather than leaking an executor thread + child git process.
evidence: voter.py:497-499: `await asyncio.to_thread(gc.clone_change_ref, ...)` — blocking work runs in a thread

gerrit_client.py:300-306: `subprocess.run([..."git"..."fetch"...], check=True, capture_output=True, text=True)` has NO `timeout=` argument, so a hung git fetch blocks the executor thread forever even after the coroutine is cancelled
commit message: the hang mode is 'a clone/subprocess/LLM call blocked indefinitely (as during the disk-full incident)' — exactly the to_thread/subprocess path that wait_for cannot interrupt
advisory.finding: asyncio.wait_for cancels only the awaiting worker coroutine; the actual hung work runs inside asyncio.to_thread on the default ThreadPoolExecutor (e.g. clone_change_ref's subprocess.run, which has no timeout=), so on the incident's own root cause (blocking git in a thread under disk-full) the hung thread and child git process are NOT stopped — they leak. Each timed-out review permanently consumes one executor worker; under sustained hangs the bounded default executor (min(32, cpu+4)) can exhaust, at which point new to_thread submissions queue behind dead threads and the worker stalls again — the exact failure this fix aims to prevent, one level deeper.
advisory.suggested_fix: Give the git subprocess.run calls in clone_change_ref an explicit timeout= (raising GerritError on TimeoutExpired), and/or bound/replace the to_thread executor so a cancelled review does not permanently leak a worker thread.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap the reconciler's review_and_vote call in the same bounded timeout as the worker so a persistently-hanging review cannot wedge the reconcile loop.
evidence: reconcile.py:215: `result = await _voter.review_and_vote(ev, config=cfg, gerrit=gc, dedup=store)` — no wait_for wrapper

app.py:160-171: timeout handler comment '(fail-closed: the change simply goes un-voted this pass and is picked up by the backfill reconciler)' and error text 'the backfill reconciler will retry' — the fix's recovery story depends on the reconciler
reconcile.py:247-249: reconcile_loop awaits reconcile_once serially; a hung review inside it blocks all further passes
advisory.finding: The backfill reconciler — the recovery path the abandoned-review handler explicitly relies on ('the backfill reconciler will retry') — invokes review_and_vote WITHOUT any asyncio.wait_for timeout, so it retains the identical unbounded-hang vulnerability the ticket describes. A change that hangs on every review (e.g. persistent disk-full) will hang the reconcile_once await, stalling the whole reconcile loop.
advisory.suggested_fix: Apply asyncio.wait_for(_voter.review_and_vote(...), timeout=_review_timeout_seconds()) in reconcile_once, or factor the timed review into a shared helper used by both the worker and the reconciler.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test where the fake review blocks synchronously inside asyncio.to_thread (mimicking the real subprocess hang) to prove the mitigation behaves correctly for the incident's actual hang mode.
evidence: test_review_bot.py:1128: `await asyncio.Event().wait()  # never returns — simulates the hung clone/LLM`
test comment claims it simulates 'the hung clone/subprocess/LLM' but Event().wait() is cancellable, unlike a blocking subprocess.run in to_thread
gerrit_client.py:300-306: the real hang path is subprocess.run without timeout in a worker thread, which wait_for cannot cancel
advisory.finding: The new test simulates a hang with `await asyncio.Event().wait()` — a cooperative, immediately-cancellable pure-async await — rather than a blocking subprocess.run running under asyncio.to_thread (the real incident's hang mode). The test therefore only proves wait_for cancels a well-behaved coroutine; it gives false confidence that the timeout terminates the actual disk-full hang, which is non-cancellable executor work.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a table-driven test for _review_timeout_seconds() covering missing/empty, unparseable, zero, and negative values all returning DEFAULT_REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, plus a valid positive value passing through.
evidence: docstring at app.py:72-73: 'A missing / unparseable / non-positive value falls back to the default (a 0 or negative timeout would abandon every review immediately)'

search_files over tests/unit/test_review_bot.py for 'REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS' returns only the single monkeypatch.setenv('0.05') at test line 1127 (a valid positive value) — no test asserts the missing/unparseable/non-positive fallbacks return the default
ABSENCE: no test invokes appmod._review_timeout_seconds() directly; regression removing/loosening the `val <= 0` or ValueError guards would not be caught
advisory.finding: The new _review_timeout_seconds() env parser has three fallback branches (missing/empty, unparseable ValueError, non-positive <= 0) that are entirely untested; the only test exercising the env var passes a valid positive float ('0.05'). In particular the `val <= 0` guard carries real fail-closed behavior — per the docstring, a 0/negative timeout would make asyncio.wait_for abandon EVERY review immediately — so a regression weakening that guard would silently break the whole gate with no failing test.
scenarios: Operator sets REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=0 or a negative value
advisory.suggested_fix: Add a parametrized test asserting appmod._review_timeout_seconds() returns float(DEFAULT_REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) for env values None, '', '0', '-5', 'abc', and returns 300.0 for '300'.
